coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dot-gated multifunctional mesoporous silica nanocarriers for redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted and controlled drug delivery and real-time bioimaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ying
Cui, Yu
Zhao, Yating
He, Bing
Shi, Xiaoli
Di, Donghua
Zhang, Qiang
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: A distinctive and personalized nanocarrier is described here for controlled and targeted antitumor drug delivery and real-time bioimaging by combining a redox/enzyme dual-responsive disulfide-conjugated carbon dot with mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN-SS-CDHA). The carbon dot with controlling and targeting abilities was prepared through a polymerizing reaction by applying citric acid and HA as starting materials (named CDHA). The as-prepared MSN-SS-CDHA exhibited not only superior photostability and excellent biocompatibility, but also the ability to target A549 cells with overexpression of CD44 receptors. Upon loading the antitumor drug, doxorubicin (DOX), into the mesoporous channels of MSN nanoparticles, CDHA with a diameter size of 3nm completely blocked the pore entrance of DOX-encapsulated MSN nanoparticles with a pore size of about 3nm, thus preventing the premature leakage of DOX and increasing the antitumor activity until being triggered by specific stimuli in the tumor environment. The results of the cell imaging and cytotoxicity studies demonstrated that the redox/enzyme dual-responsive DOX-encapsulated MSN-SS-CDHA nanoparticles can selectively deliver and control the release of DOX into tumor cells. Ex vivo fluorescence images showed a much stronger fluorescence of MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX in the tumor site than in normal tissues, greatly facilitating the accumulation of DOX in the target tissue. However, its counterpart, MSN-SH-DOX exhibited no or much lower tumor cytotoxicity and drug accumulation in tumor tissue. In addition, MSN-SS-CD was also used as a control to investigate the ability of MSN-SS-CDHA to target A549 cells. The results obtained indicated that MSN-SS-CDHA possessed a higher cellular uptake through the CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis compared with MSN-SS-CD in the A549 cells. Such specific redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted nanocarriers are a useful strategy achieving selective controlled and targeted delivery of therapeutic reagents with real-time bioimaging, and may also facilitate the development of drug delivery systems for a number of clinical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dot
Mesoporous silica nanocarriers
Redox/enzyme dual-responsive
Targeted and controlled drug delivery
Real-time bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: European Journal of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading and in vitro stimuli-responsive drug release
Preparation and characterization of multifunctional MSN nanoparticles
Cell CLSM imaging and FCS of MSN-SS-CDHA for redox/enzyme-responsive targeting delivery
Controlled release of DOX and release mechanism studies from redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanocarriers in buffer solutions
Targeted cell viability via the redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanoparticles
Biodistribution of different DOX formulations in tumor-bearing mice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDHA. The inset image is the size distribution of CDHA. (B) Highlighted areas of A. (C) The fast Fourier transform pattern of B, which shows crystalline structures of CDHA. (D) TEM images of redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted MSN-SS-CDHA nanoparticles.

(A) UV/vis absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of CDHA in aqueous solutions. The insets are photos of CDHA in aqueous solution under visible (left) and 365nm UV (right) illumination. (B and C) PL spectra of CDHA under different excitation wavelengths with the concentration of 1mg/mL. (D) The PL spectra of CDHA with the concentration of 200μg/mL in the different dispersion media under excitation of 370nm.

(A) The FTIR spectra of as-prepared CDHA, MSN-SH, MSN-SS-NH2, and MSN-SS-CDHA in the solid state. (B) Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of MSN-SH, MSN-SS-NH2, and MSN-SS-CDHA. (C) Zeta potentials of different samples.

(A) Unadsorbed BSA amount of MSN-SH and MSN-SS-CDHA. Hemolysis percentage (B) and photographs of RBCs (C) by MSN-SH and MSN-SS-CDHA. (D) Cell viability of NIH-3T3 and A549 cells by MTT assays after incubation with MSN-SH and MSN-SS-CDHA for 48h. Data are expressed as mean±SD (n=3). ***p<0.001.

CLSM images of NIH-3T3 and A549 cell lines pretreated with MSN-SS-CD and MSN-SS-CDHA for 2h at 37°C or 4°C, under excitation of 405, 488, and 561nm. The reflected signal of MSN samples was also observed under excitation at 561nm. The CD44 receptor inhibitor, HA, was used to competitively bind CD44 receptor. The scale bar is 25μm.
Mean fluorescent intensities of NIH-3T3 (A) and A549 (B) cells treated with MSN-SS-CD and MSN-SS-CDHA for 2h. Data are expressed as mean±SD (n=3). *p<0.05, **p<0.01 and ***p<0.001.
DOX release profiles from DOX-encapsulated nanocarriers upon GSH and HAase treatment at pH 5.5 PBS (A) and pH 7.4 PBS (B).

The cell viability of DOX-loaded MSN nanoparticles at different concentrations of DOX in (A) NIH-3T3 cells and (B) A549 cells for 24h and 48h. Free DOX was used as contrast. Data are expressed as mean±SD (n=5).
Ex vivo imaging of heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor after i.v. injecting free DOX, MSN-SH-DOX, and MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX into the tumor-bearing mice.
Schematic illustration of the redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted MSN-SS-CDHA nanoconjugates for controlled of targeted drug delivery and real-time bioimaging of tumor cells.
A synthetic route and possible synthetic mechanism of CDHA by condensation, polymerization, and carbonization using CD, EA, and HA as the starting materials.
IC50 values of different DOX formulations against NIH-3T3 and A549 cells.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dot-gated multifunctional mesoporous silica nanocarriers for redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted and controlled drug delivery and real-time bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Peking University, Beijing 100191, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Natural and Biomimetic Drugs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A distinctive and personalized nanocarrier is described here for controlled and targeted antitumor drug delivery and real-time bioimaging by combining a redox/enzyme dual-responsive disulfide-conjugated carbon dot with mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN-SS-CDHA). The carbon dot with controlling and targeting abilities was prepared through a polymerizing reaction by applying citric acid and HA as starting materials (named CDHA). The as-prepared MSN-SS-CDHA exhibited not only superior photostability and excellent biocompatibility, but also the ability to target A549 cells with overexpression of CD44 receptors. Upon loading the antitumor drug, doxorubicin (DOX), into the mesoporous channels of MSN nanoparticles, CDHA with a diameter size of 3nm completely blocked the pore entrance of DOX-encapsulated MSN nanoparticles with a pore size of about 3nm, thus preventing the premature leakage of DOX and increasing the antitumor activity until being triggered by specific stimuli in the tumor environment. The results of the cell imaging and cytotoxicity studies demonstrated that the redox/enzyme dual-responsive DOX-encapsulated MSN-SS-CDHA nanoparticles can selectively deliver and control the release of DOX into tumor cells. Ex vivo fluorescence images showed a much stronger fluorescence of MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX in the tumor site than in normal tissues, greatly facilitating the accumulation of DOX in the target tissue. However, its counterpart, MSN-SH-DOX exhibited no or much lower tumor cytotoxicity and drug accumulation in tumor tissue. In addition, MSN-SS-CD was also used as a control to investigate the ability of MSN-SS-CDHA to target A549 cells. The results obtained indicated that MSN-SS-CDHA possessed a higher cellular uptake through the CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis compared with MSN-SS-CD in the A549 cells. Such specific redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted nanocarriers are a useful strategy achieving selective controlled and targeted delivery of therapeutic reagents with real-time bioimaging, and may also facilitate the development of drug delivery systems for a number of clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the leading causes of mortality worldwide and cancer-causing events continue to increase dramatically [1]. Currently, cancer treatment mainly focuses on systemic chemotherapy to eliminate the isolated cancer cells within normal tissues. However, the lack of tumor specificity, systemic toxicity, and the emergence of multidrug resistance severely limit the practical applications of chemotherapy in clinical situations [2]. In order to improve the therapeutic efficacy and reduce side effect, targeted drug delivery systems have been developed in recent decades [3–5]. However, most of the current research has mainly focused on the intracellular delivery of nanocarriers into the cytoplasm, which ignores the premature leaking of therapeutic agents prior to reaching their target site [6–8]. Therefore, an ideal design for use in clinical practice would allow targeted treatment by delivering therapeutic agents into tumor cells without premature drug leakage.

In recent years, mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MSN) with a pore size ranging from 2nm to 50nm have been developed as a promising carrier for drug delivery systems by virtue of their high specific surface area, adjustable particle size, excellent mesoporous structure, and good biocompatibility. In addition, multifunctional MSN with controlling and targeting abilities have been developed mainly due to their easily modified surface allowing conjugation with a range of compounds [9–13]. More importantly, MSN can also serve as a support for combination with magnetic or luminescent materials for diagnosis. Currently, a variety of MSN-based drug delivery systems have been developed by applying linear molecules, nanoparticles, macrocyclic compounds and multilayers as “gatekeepers” over the pore entrance of MSN, which can be stimulated by changes in pH or temperature, redox potentials, enzymes or photoirradiation to release drugs on demand [14–18]. Although some stimuli-responsive drug release systems are well-designed, we are still a long way from the development of practical applications due to the apparent differences between normal tissues and tumor sites. Among the various potential stimuli, the redox potential is especially appealing as an internal stimulus due to the significant difference in the level of glutathione (GSH) between the extracellular (2μM) and intracellular (10mM) environments. Moreover, the intracellular GSH concentration in most tumor cells is at least 4-fold higher than that in normal cells [19,20]. Following redox potential stimulus, the gatekeepers allow the drug release unless drug loading system is exposed to the high level of GSH.

As a new class of fluorescent carbon-based materials, carbon dots (CDs) with diameters up to 10nm have recently attracted increasing attention in fields such as bioimaging, clinical diagnosis and catalysis [21–25]. CDs with the graphitic structure have a similar physico-chemical features of semiconductor quantum dots. Compared with the traditional heavy-metal-based quantum dots (QDs) and organic dyes, CDs exhibit superior properties including nontoxicity, good water solubility, superior photostability, excellent cell membrane permeability, good biocompatibility and adjustable surface functional groups and, so, are excellent candidates for fluorescence bioimaging [25–27]. In previous reports, CDs were always used as a bioimaging agent instead of a control for drug delivery. Considering that their diameter ranges from 2nm to 10nm, CDs can be used as gatekeepers to cap the entrance of MSN to control the on-demand drug release. Recently, we synthesized a nontoxic, highly fluorescent N-doped CD nanomaterial with self-targeting ability via a simple one-step route, which has received little attention until now. The N-doped means that this carbon dots have been doped with nitrogen source like ethylenediamine and ammonia, which can enhance the quantum yields as reported [28]. In particular, the self-targeting CD (called as CDHA), possessing a targeting property since they can bind the CD44 receptor which is overexpressed in the tumor cells, has been prepared by polymerizing reactions, in which the citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EA) were used as starting materials to form a branching monomer that subsequently reacts with polymer hyaluronic acid (HA) (Scheme 2). We are interested in HA polymers since they have a specific interaction with CD44 receptors that are overexpressed on tumor cells [29–32]. In addition, compared with normal tissues HA can be easily hydrolyzed by hyaluronidases (HAase) that are abundant in the tumor issues [33,34]. So, this self-targeting N-doped CD also exhibits an enzyme-responsive action.

In this work, we describe the design of a multifunctional antitumor drug delivery system involving the introduction of an enzyme-responsive self-targeting CDHA onto the disulfide bond modified MSN surface (MSN-SS-CDHA). This allows the targeted delivery and controlled release of the loaded DOX into tumor cells, simultaneously with real-time bioimaging (Scheme 1 ). Generally, MSN-SS-CDHA nanocarriers are inactive in normal cells without/or less glutathione (GSH) and HAase expression, however, after reaching the targeted tumor cells and tissues where there is a high concentration of GSH and HAase, the specific enzymatic cleavage of the disulfide bonds can selectively remove the N-doped CDHA from the surface of MSN and the encapsulated DOX can be released. This allows improved antitumor treatment and minimizes side effects. More importantly, apart from its selective targeting and controlled drug delivery, this MSN-SS-CDHA also offers great potential for real-time imaging to monitor cellular tracking during tumor therapy.

3-Mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS), 2,2′-dipyridyl disulfide, glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), hyaluronidase (HAase, ≥300U/mg), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDCI) and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), Ethylenediamine (EA), Cetyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB) and Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) were obtained from Shan Dong Yu Wang Reagent Company (China). Cell culture medium dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM), RPMI 1640, fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25%) and penicillin-streptomycin were obtained from GIBCO, Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, U.S.A.). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was supplied by Amreso (Solon, OH, U.S.A.). All other chemicals were of analytical grade as required and used without further purification.

The thiol functionalized MSN (MSN-SH) nanoparticles were prepared according to the previous reported method [35]. Firstly, CTAB (1g) was dissolved in 480mL of deionized water, and then 3.5mL 2M NaOH was added to the above solution. After the mixture was heated to 80°C, 5.0mL TEOS was added dropwise to the CTAB solution. Then, the reaction solution was kept under stirring for 2h and then centrifugated to collect the resulting precipitate. Then, the introduction of thiol was conducted by redispersing as-prepared MSN nanoparticles in absolute ethanol, followed by the addition of MPTMS (1mL). The mixture was then refluxed for 12h. For template extraction, NH4NO3 (0.80g) was subsequently added and stirred overnight at 80°C to remove the surfactant, and then the MSN-SH was centrifuged, washed with ethanol three times. After that, an ATS AH110D homogenizer (ATS Engineer Inc., Shanghai, China) was performed to homogenize the resulting precipitate. The MSN-SS-NH2 was synthesized by using the disulfide bond exchange reaction. The MSN-SH (100mg) and Py-SS-NH2 (25mg) were dispersed in anhydrous ethanol (10mL) and stirred at room temperature for 36h. The resulting MSN-SS-NH2 was isolated by centrifugation and dried overnight under vacuum.

Typically, citric acid (CA, 1.875g), hyaluronic acid (HA, 0.225g) and Ethylenediamine (EA, 3.75mL) were added into a beaker. The reaction mixture was sufficiently mixed via sonication and heated to 120°C and kept for 3h. The final reaction products were dialyzed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut off: 3500) against water for 24h to remove small molecules. The water was changed every 4h, and then CDHA in the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain the brown solid. By contrast, another kind of CD was also prepared from citric acid (CA, 1.875g) without HA via the same hydrothermal method under 120°C for 3h. Then, the above product CDHA/CD was activated using EDC and NHS for 0.5h and subsequently MSN-SS-NH2 was added to the above solution under stirring for 12h. Then the resultant MSN-SS-CDHA/MSN-SS-CD was collected by centrifugation, washed extensively with distilled water and finally dried under vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and in vitro stimuli-responsive drug release
ce:section.ce:para: Before capping, MSN-SH (80mg) was stirred with a PBS 7.4 solution of DOX (2.5mg/mL, 8mL) for 12h at room temperature. Then, the DOX-encapsulated MSN-SH (MSN-SH-DOX) was isolated by centrifugation and dried under vacuum and the CDHA capped MSN-SH-DOX nanoparticles (MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX) were prepared as described (see Section 2.3 for details). To confirm the redox and enzyme dual-stimuli responsive release feature, DOX release from MSN nanoparticles was performed at the different pH values (pH 5.0 and pH 7.4 PBS) with/without GSH and HAase. The prepared DOX-loaded MSN nanoparticle samples were suspended in the release medium and shaken at 120rpm (37°C). At specified times, 3mL samples of release medium were withdrawn and replaced with an equal volume of fresh medium. The release of DOX was determined at 480nm by UV–vis spectrophotometry (UV-2000, UNICO, U.S.A.).

The BSA protein adsorption was measured according to the reported method [36]. For this, 30mg BSA was dissolved in 50mL distilled water under gentle stirring. Then, 10mg MSN nanoparticle samples were dispersed into 5mL PBS solution, followed by the addition of 5mL BSA solution. The mixture was shaken at a rate of 150rpm at 37°C for 4h. The mixture solutions were then centrifuged, and the supernatant was collected. The residual BSA was determined by the staining method with Coomassie brilliant blue at a UV–vis absorption wavelength of 595nm. A calibration curve was drawn by using BSA solutions with a series of concentrations. BSA adsorbed on MSN and MSN-SS-PEG were calculated according to
BSA
adsorbance
( % ) = ( Cb - Ce ) ∗ 6 ∗ V / m
where Cb and Ce were initial BSA concentration in a control group and the residual BSA concentration after BSA adsorption; V was the total solution volume (10mL); m was the weight of MSN samples added into solutions.

Rabbit red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by centrifugation and then washed with sterile normal saline. The RBCs were diluted with saline to 2/100 of their volume and then 2mL of the diluted cell suspension was mixed with 2mL MSN nanoparticle samples at the different concentrations ranging from 20 to 1000μg/mL. After standing for 4h, the mixtures were centrifuged and the absorbance of the supernatant was recorded on a UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer at 541nm. The hemolysis of the RBCs in saline and distilled water was used as a positive and negative control, respectively.

A549 cells and NIH-3T3 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 2.0×104 and incubated in cell medium for 24h. The medium was then removed and replaced with blank nanoparticles and DOX-loaded samples at different concentrations. After incubation, 20μL MTT solutions (5mg/mL) were added to the culture medium and incubated for an additional 4h at 37°C to measure the cell viability. After removing the culture medium containing MTT, 150µL DMSO was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals formed. Finally, the absorbance of the formazan was measured at 490nm using a microplate reader.

The cellular uptake of MSN nanoparticle samples by A549 and NIH-3T3 cells was investigated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). Each of the cell samples was seeded in sterile glass bottomed dishes at a density of 105 cells per well and incubated for 24h. Then, the cells were treated with MSN nanoparticle samples (100μg/mL) for 1h at 37°C, the supernatant was removed and the cells were washed with cold PBS. Then, the cells were fixed with 4% formaldehyde for 10min at room temperature and washed with cold PBS again. Finally, the cells were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

The quantitative cellular uptake of MSN samples was investigated by flow cytometry scanning (FCS). Each cell line was seeded in 24-well plates at a density of 5×104 cells per well and allowed to adhere overnight. The cells were then treated with MSN samples for 1h at 37°C followed by washing with cold PBS and trypsin treatment. Then, the cells were centrifuged for 5min at 1600rpm and resuspended in 400μL PBS. Data for 10,000 gated cells were collected and the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) was measured by FCS. In the competition studies, the A549 cells were incubated with 200μg/mL HA for 30min before the nanoparticles were added.

Nude mice (n=3) were placed onto a stereotaxic device and 100μL PBS containing 5×105 A549 cells was slowly injected into the right flank of each mouse. Twenty days later, the tumor-bearing mice were found to have developed 200mm3 tumors. Then, the mice were given an i.v. administration of the same amount of DOX (1mg DOX/kg body weight) as free DOX, MSN-SH-DOX, and MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX through the tail vein. Four hours later, the mice were sacrificed, and the tissue distribution was observed by ex vivo fluorescence imaging at an excitation wavelength of 470nm and an emission wavelength of 690nm to measure the DOX fluorescence. The animal protocols were approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee of this University.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a Tecnai G2 20 instrument (FEI, U.S.A.) and IR spectra were recorded on a Bruker IFS 55 (Switzerland). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were recorded on a SA3100 surface area analyzer (Beckman Coulter, U.S.A.) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D/Max-2500PC X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Multifunctional microplate reader SpectraMax M5 (Molecular Devices, U.S.A.).

The data were expressed as mean±standard deviation (SD) and statistical differences between the different experimental and control groups were determined by Student’s t-test. Differences were considered statistically significant with p<0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of multifunctional MSN nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: As a proof of concept, the synthetic process of redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanocarriers, illustrated in Scheme 1, was used to selectively control the targeted drug delivery and real-time bioimaging. The synthesis of MSN-SS-NH2 was carried out as reported previously. Following the disulfide bond exchange reaction, MSN-SS-NH2 was produced which can facilitate further surface modification for biomedical applications. In order to achieve dual-responsive targeted antitumor drug release, a self-targeting CDHA was first prepared by the hydrothermal method, and consisted of two units including HA polymer for targeting, and a printing mold to block the mesopores of MSN for sensitively controlled drug delivery. Typically, the carboxyl groups of citric acid and HA were first polymerized with amine to form amide polymers, then the polymers were further carbonized to generate CDHA (Scheme 2 ). Finally, the prepared self-targeting CDHA was conjugated with disulfide-functionalized MSN nanoparticles. Following the specific condensation reaction between the COOH of CDHA and the NH2 of MSN-SS-NH2, redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanoconjugates with controlling, targeting and bioimaging ability were finally produced.

The as-synthesized CDHA produced brown-red colored homogeneous solutions at room temperature over a few weeks. A variety of different reaction conditions were investigated in the preparation of CDHA and the optimal formulation is shown in Table S1. Considering the preferable quantum yields and the self-targeting and controlling ability of CDHA, 120°C and 3.75mL EA were chosen as the optimal reaction conditions. Fig. 1 A shows the TEM images of CDHA that possesses a uniform diameter of 3.0nm. The high resolution TEM images in Fig. 1B shows a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21nm, which is in agreement with the basal spacing of graphite carbon. The observed lattice in the FFT image also suggested that CDHA has a CDHA are crystalline structure (Fig. 1C). The XRD patterns of CDHA (Fig. S1) also had a broad peak centered at 21.5°, which is attributed to highly amorphous carbon atoms. In the UV vis absorption spectrum, three wide absorption bands at 230, 275 and 360nm of CDHA were observed in Fig. 2 A, which corresponded to the π-π∗ transition of multiple conjugate chromophores and the n-π∗ transition of carbonyl groups, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2A, CDHA had excitation and emission wavelengths at 350nm and 450nm and displayed a strong blue color 1 For interpretation of color in Figs. 2 and 5, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 under a 365nm UV lamp with a relative fluorescence quantum yield of 39.6% (with quinine sulfate as a reference). The PL spectra showed an excitation-dependent behavior with the highest emission wavelength shifting from 450nm to 530nm when the excitation wavelength was red-shifted from 330nm to 470nm (in Fig. 2B and C). The excitation-dependent behavior gave CDHA multicolor cell and real-time bioimaging ability. Fig. 2D shows that the PL intensity of CDHA dissolved in different media, indicating that the photoluminescence fluorescence is also can be used in further studies. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 3 A) were used to identify the surface modification of MSN nanoparticles. In the CDHA spectra, the broad bands from 3025 to 3700cm−1 were attributed to hydroxyl and amino stretching vibrations. The vibrational bands at 1583, 1665, and 1333cm−1 demonstrated the presence of NH, CO, and CC groups on the surface of CDHA. In addition, a minor peak at 2560cm−1 was observed in the spectrum of MSN-SH, confirming the presence of the SH group. However, the minor peak at 2560cm−1 had disappeared in the spectrum of MSN-SS-NH2, indicating the successful preparation of MSN-SS-NH2. The grafting of CDHA on the surface of MSN-SS-NH2 was confirmed by the vibrational band at 1583cm−1 attributed to the amide bond in the spectrum of MSN-SS-CDHA. The zeta potential of CDHA was about −20.9mV, indicating that there were a lot of COOH groups on the surface that could further facilitate the dispersing of MSN nanoparticles in the medium. The zeta potential of MSN-SH was around −19.6mV while, after modification of Py-SS-NH2, the zeta potential became a positive value of +38.1mV. Then, when the CDHA was grafted onto the MSN-SS-NH2, the zeta potential significantly decreased to +6.66mV, further confirming the successful modification of CDHA on the MSN nanoparticles (Fig. 3C). The TEM image in Fig. 1D shows the narrow size distribution of MSN-SS-CDHA with a particle diameter of 100nm, which is suitable size for obtaining effective cell penetration and targeted drug delivery. The adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution of MSN-SH, MSN-SS-NH2, and MSN-SS-CDHA are shown in the Fig. 3B, which can be seen to decrease markedly in surface area and pore volume after further functionalized grafting.

As an effective drug delivery carrier, biocompatibility studies are essential before being used in patients. Therefore, the BSA protein adsorption, hemolysis and the cell viability was investigated to identify the biocompatibility for practical applications. The BSA protein adsorption of MSN-SH and MSN-SS-CDHA was measured as shown in Fig. 4 A; after modifying the CDHA, the absorbance of the nanoparticles was sharply reduced from 22.9% (MSN-SH) to 3.5% (MSN-SS-CDHA), indicating that the grafting of CDHA can reduce protein adsorption and prolong the blood circulation time.

The hemolytic behavior of MSN samples was studied using a previously reported method. The percentage hemolysis and images of MSN-SH and MSN-SS-CDHA over a concentration ranging from 20 to 1000μg/mL are shown in Fig. 4B. At a higher concentration of MSN-SH (500 and 1000μg/mL), the percentage hemolysis was about 10.0% and 25%, respectively, which increased the securityrisks associated with the practical application in a drug delivery system. Fortunately, the decoration of CDHA on the surface of MSN-SH can reduce the electrostatic interactions and affinity between the silica and the membrane of the red blood cells. As expected, the percentage hemolysis of MSN-SS-CDHA decreased sharply to 1% even at the high concentration of 1000μg/mL, indicating the improved biocompatibility of the MSN nanoparticles.

The cytotoxicity of MSN nanoparticles was studied to confirm that the as-prepared drug nanocarriers are suitable for biomedical applications. The cell viability was measured using the MTT assay with A549 and NIH-3T3 cells. In this study, the different cell lines were incubated with different concentrations of MSN nanoparticles, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 4D, MSN-SH exhibited a slight degree of toxicity at concentrations of 10–100μg/mL after a 48h culture. However, all of the cell viability rates exceeded 76% after incubation with MSN-SS-CDHA for 48h at different concentrations, confirming that MSN-SS-CDHA nanocarriers with good biocompatibility were suitable for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell CLSM imaging and FCS of MSN-SS-CDHA for redox/enzyme-responsive targeting delivery
ce:section.ce:para: CLSM and FCS were used to further demonstrate the feasibility of the use of controlled and targeted MSN-SS-CDHA nanoparticles for the delivery into cells. The tumor cell line A549 with over-expressed CD44 receptors was chosen as a target cell and NIH-3T3 cells with negative receptors were used as a control. Both A549 and NIH-3T3 cells were incubated with MSN nanoconjugates (100μg/mL) cell culture medium for 2h to obtain effective cell imaging. MSN-SS-CD without targeting ability was used as a negative group. The cellular uptake was investigated by monitoring of the fluorescence of carbon dots coated on the MSN nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 5 , after 2h incubation, the cell-internalized carbon dot-modified MSN nanoparticles emitted blue, green, and red fluorescence under an excitation wavelength of 405, 488, and 561nm, respectively. Some researchers have reported that when the metal nanoparticles were excited with a specific laser beam, CLSM could detect the reflected laser signal at the same wavelength [37]. In this study, the sharp reflected signal of MSN samples was also observed under an excitation wavelength of 561nm and the detector was tuned at 553–565nm. In addition, the differential interference contrast (DIC) microscopy was used to image the cells. As shown in the Figs. 5 and 6 , when the NIH-3T3 and A549 cells were cultured with MSN-SS-CDHA, the fluorescence signal of the A549 cells was much stronger than that of NIH-3T3 cells, indicating greater uptake of MSN-SS-CDHA by A549 cells than that by NIH-3T3 cells. In contrast, almost no significant difference between NIH-3T3 and A549 cells was detected when the MSN-SS-CD nanocarriers were incubated with these two cell lines, indicating that MSN-SS-CD did not have the ability to target A549 cells. However, unlike the fluorescence in cytoplasm as found in NIH-3T3 cells, the fluorescent signals in A549 cells co-cultured with MSN-SS-CDHA were higher than that co-cultured with MSN-SS-CD. These cellular imaging results confirmed that the redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanoconjugates could more easily enter the receptors of over-expressed A549 tumor cells than the negative NIH-3T3 cells. In addition, to further demonstrate the selectivity of MSN-SS-CDHA mediated by CD44 receptors, the similar cellular incubation with HA, an efficient CD44-receptor inhibitor, was also conducted using A549 cells. The fluorescence intensity was reduced sharply compared with similar A549 cell incubation but without inhibitor pretreatment, indicating that the internalization of MSN-SS-CDHA was mainly mediated by CD44 receptors. Moreover, relatively faint fluorescence signals were found in the A549 cells incubated with MSN-SS-CDHA at 4°C, suggesting that the uptake of MSN-SS-CDHA by A549 cells involves energy-dependent endocytosis. In order to further examine the uptake of MSN samples by each cell line, flow cytometry was utilized to measure the fluorescence. The endocytosis of MSN-SS-CD and MSN-SS-CDHA in NIH-3T3 and A549 cells is shown in Fig. 6A and B, respectively, in agreement with the CLSM results and further demonstrating the high targeting ability of MSN-SS-CDHA for A549 cells. Therefore, MSN-SS-CDHA can serve as a promising targeting nanocarrier with an attractive bioimaging feature for A549 cells with over-expressed CD44 receptors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Controlled release of DOX and release mechanism studies from redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanocarriers in buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The redox/enzyme dual-responsive controlled release of DOX from the drug-loaded MSN-SS-CDHA delivery system was studied by analysis of the absorbance of DOX at 480nm in the presence or absence of GSH and HAase under different pH conditions at 37°C. As shown in Fig. 7 , there was a relatively fast DOX release from MSN-SH-DOX nanoparticles at different pH values. However, MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX nanoparticles exhibited a limited DOX release in the absence of GSH and HAase at pH 5.0 and 7.4, confirming the good capping ability of the disulfide bond conjugated CDHA. Moreover, accelerated DOX release from MSN-SS-CDHA could be observed under prolonged GSH or HAase treatment due to cleavage of the disulfide bond or the surface degradation of CDHA. In the presence of GSH, the disulfide bonds are reduced and cleaved, which allows opening of the pore entrance of MSN nanoparticles and on-demand DOX release from MSN nanoparticles. Following treatment with HAase, the outer layer of CDHA was degraded by HAase, which also allowed some release of DOX from the mesoporous channels. Furthermore, the cumulative DOX release was further improved by simultaneous treatment with GSH and HAase. In addition, the DOX release was higher in pH 5.0 PBS than in pH 7.4 PBS, which was attributed to the strong electrostatic interaction between DOX and silica under a neutral pH environment [38].

In order to investigate the DOX release mechanism, the zeta potential and PL intensity of MSN-SS-CDHA were measured at predetermined times in the presence of HAase. MSN-SS-NH2 has a highly positive value of +38.1mV, while the zeta potential was reduced gradually to +6.66mV after grafting the negative charged CDHA through an amidation reaction. Following treatment of HAase, the zeta potential of MSN-SS-CDHA was increased gradually from +6.66 to +20mV over time, indicating that the negatively charged CDHA was degraded from the surface of MSN-SS-CDHA (Fig. S2). To further investigate the release mechanism, the release medium and the residual MSN-SS-CDHA precipitate in the presence of HAase were collected and measured using a multifunctional microplate reader. The results obtained showed that the PL intensity of the release medium gradually increased and, accordingly, the PL intensity of the residual precipitate was reduced over time, demonstrating the degradation of CDHA on the surface of MSN-SS-CDHA which induced DOX release on demand.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted cell viability via the redox/enzyme dual-responsive MSN-SS-CDHA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The encouraging results of the cell imaging and in vitro DOX controlled release studies prompted us to further evaluate the antitumor ability of the prepared MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX, MSN-SH-DOX, and free DOX in the different cell lines. In this study, NIH-3T3 and A549 cells were used and incubated with DOX-loaded MSN nanoparticles and free DOX, respectively, and the cell viability was measured using an MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 8 , the cytotoxicity of different DOX preparations was time- and concentration-dependent for the different cell lines. For example, the different concentrations of free DOX incubated with A549 and NIH-3T3 cells for 48h are more cytotoxic than for 24h. When 1μg/mL DOX was treated, the cell viability for A549 and NIH-3T3 cells was found to be 36% and 41%, respectively. Greater cytotoxicity to these cells was observed at higher DOX concentrations. Compared with free DOX, incubation of different concentrations of DOX-loaded MSN-SS-CDHA and MSN-SH nanoparticles with A549 and NIH-3T3 cells was also conducted to evaluate the antitumor activity. Under the same DOX concentration, DOX-loaded MSN-SS-CDHA showed a higher antitumor activity in CD44 receptor over-expressed A549 cells compared with that in NIH-3T3 cells. In addition, the MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX exhibited a more cytotoxicity than MSN-SH-DOX in A549 cells, confirming that the MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX nanoconjugates were taken up by CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis, thereby increasing the antitumor effect of DOX. However, compared with free DOX and MSN-SH-DOX that produced clear cytotoxicity, a lower cytotoxicity was observed in receptor-negative NIH-3T3 cells when MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX nanoformulations were used, further indicating the good capping effect of disulfide bond conjugated CDHA and the poor selectivity of MSN-SS-CDHA toward NIH-3T3 cells. Moreover, the IC50 for 48h was calculated using GraphPad Prism 6 software, and the results are displayed in Table 1 . When the A549 cancer cells were treated with various DOX formulations, the IC50 value was sharply reduced from 2.22μg/mL for MSN-SH-DOX to 0.62μg/mL for MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX that is similar to the value for free DOX of 0.44μg/mL, further confirming the improved antitumor ability of DOX-loaded MSN-SS-CDHA in CD44 receptor over-expressed tumor cells. However, the IC50 value of MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX increased to 3.49μg/mL following incubation with the negative NIH-3T3 cells, 2–6-fold higher than that of MSN-SH-DOX and free DOX, clearly indicating that MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX nanoformulations can reduce the side effects in normal cells. The observed cytotoxicity was more limited when the NIH-3T3 cells were incubated with MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX nanoconjugates than that with MSN-SH-DOX, further suggesting that a lack of specific GSH and HAase in this cell line would not trigger significant stimuli-responsive DOX release from MSN-SS-CDHA, thereby resulting in the limited DOX efficacy against normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution of different DOX formulations in tumor-bearing mice
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm the ability of MSN-SS-CDHA nanoconjugates to deliver DOX into tumors with minimum accumulation in normal organs, the tissue distribution of DOX in nude mice was studied after an i.v. injection of free DOX, MSN-SH-DOX, and MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX into tumor-bearing mice. An ex vivo imaging system was used to observe the tissue distribution. As shown in Fig. 9 , in the mice injected with free DOX or MSN-SH-DOX, DOX was found in the liver, but not in the tumors, 4h after the injection. However, after an injection of MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX, DOX accumulation in the liver was reduced, but increased in tumor tissues. These results suggested that the fluorescence in mice injected with MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX was much stronger in the tumor site than that of the other two groups, confirming that MSN-SS-CDHA could target the tumor sites and minimize the accumulation in other normal organs. In addition, for the MSN-SH-DOX nanoformulation, the high accumulation of DOX in the liver could be due to the uptake of nanosized MSN-SH by the reticuloendothelial system. These results suggest that the MSN-SS-CDHA nanocarriers can improve the antitumor efficacy by selectively directing chemotherapeutic drugs to tumor tissues and controlling drug release on demand.

In our present study, we have developed a distinctive drug delivery vehicle for controlled and targeted antitumor drug delivery and real-time bioimaging by integrating a GSH and HAase responsive disulfide conjugated-CDHA with mesoporous silica nanoparticles. The diameter of 3nm of CDHA completely blocks the pore entrance of DOX-encapsulated MSN nanoparticles which have a pore size of about 3nm, thus preventing premature drug leakage and increasing the antitumor activity. The results of in vitro cell imaging and cytotoxicity experiments suggested that the DOX-loaded MSN-SS-CDHA can target drug delivery to tumor cells with high CD44 receptor expression and control the drug release by a cleavable enzyme-responsive CDHA gatekeeper that can be triggered by stimulation of GSH and HAase, which are abundant in the tumor environment, thus resulting in increased tumor cytotoxicity and lower side effects. An in vivo imaging study further confirmed that MSN-SS-CDHA-DOX nanoconjugates can accumulate into tumor sites with much higher intensity compared with free DOX and MSN-SH-DOX, indicating that the prepared CDHA can be used as a targeted fluorescence imaging agent. Furthermore, it was also demonstrated that the redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted drug nanocarriers have an additional appealing function allowing the possibility of conducting real-time bioimaging of intracellular and in vivo delivery via the fluorescent CDHA gatekeeper. This is the first example of the application of polymer HA combined with citric acid to prepare polymer-like carbon dots. As reported, HA is a common targeting agent that binds to CD44 receptors in tumor cells. Until now, HA has been used to conjugate with various nanomaterials to selectively target CD44 receptor over-expressed cells. In our present work, we found that the targeting ability of CDHA caps may be attributed to the formation of HA-like functional groups that could also selectively bind to CD44 receptors on the surface of the carbon dots. Further work is underway to obtain more details of the targeting mechanism of CDHA. Such specific redox/enzyme dual-responsive targeted nanocarriers will be very useful for the selective controlled and targeted delivery of therapeutic agents with real-time bioimaging, and may also facilitate the development of new drug delivery systems for clinical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program) (No. 2015CB932100), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81473165) and the Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory of Drug Preparation Design & Evaluation of Liaoning Provincial Education Department (No. LZ2015068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ejpb.2017.03.019.
